title: Are LLM merge rates not getting better?
description: A detailed Hacker News discussion from March 10, 2026 exploring whether LLM coding quality has plateaued. Key insights: 1) Models improved dramatically in 2025 with better context management and terminal-based apps like Claude Code, but code quality remains problematic with quadruple-nested control flow and unsound architecture. 2) Developers debate whether LLMs are actually getting better or if improvements come from better tooling and 'harness engineering' rather than intrinsic model improvements. 3) Discussion of 'emergent abilities mirage' - metrics may not capture real-world progress. 4) Consensus that Opus 4.5/4.6 represented a significant step change, but newer models show diminishing returns.
author: Hacker News Community

title: If AI writes code, should the session be part of the commit?
description: Hacker News discussion from March 9, 2026 about version control practices for AI-generated code. Key questions: Should AI coding sessions be recorded in commits? How do we attribute AI contributions? Should prompt context be included in commit metadata? Discussion explores the implications for code review, attribution, legal compliance, and understanding how code was generated. As AI writes more code, traditional version control practices may need updating.
author: Hacker News Community

title: You need to rewrite your CLI for AI agents
description: A March 8, 2026 Hacker News discussion (136 comments) about adapting command-line interfaces for AI agents. As AI coding tools become more prevalent, traditional CLIs need redesign to be more agent-friendly. Discussion covers topics like structured output, better error handling, state management, and designing interfaces that both humans and AI agents can use effectively. This reflects the growing realization that software tooling needs to evolve for the AI-agentic era.
author: Hacker News Community
